feed.title: Huandi Chen (陈欢迪)
feed.subtitle: MSc Cognitive Neuroscience student at Radboud University.

entry.title: Two Cows
entry.content:
Every Saturday morning, I go for a walk around Botersnijdersven in Nijmegen. Over time, I have become acquainted with these two cows.

Our relationship is relatively simple: we look at each other for about twenty minutes, and then they return to eating grass.

I often wonder how they process information. Their eyes stay so still — not looking forward, not looking back — as if they are quietly receiving the world without needing to react to it immediately.

And of course, if you are also interested in this topic, feel free to follow my future research on Cowgnition.

Read more

Journal of Dairy Research.

entry.title: Looking Up
entry.content:
Whenever I arrive in a new place, I like to record its sky: at different times of the day, under different kinds of weather — although, of course, taking photos of the sky on rainy days can be rather difficult.

Sometimes, I simply stop and look up. The sky feels wide, poetic, and spacious.

Skies in Edinburgh, Geneva, and Nijmegen.
entry.summary: One third of my photo album is probably made of clouds and skies

entry.title: Salad
entry.content:
Written, quite clearly, in 35°C heat: the salad was refreshing, and its colours were beautiful.

Florence was my colleague during my internship at the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), and I am very glad to have met her. She taught me French and introduced me to French culture; I taught her Chinese and shared parts of Chinese culture with her. We found that many human things are shared across languages and cultures: care, longing, empathy, and, of course, the desire to eat salad together in summer.
entry.summary: Written, quite clearly, in 35°C heat: the salad was refreshing, and its colours were beautiful.

entry.title: Colours
entry.content:
I have always loved colours, especially the way different pure colours intertwine in varying proportions, thicknesses, and intensities.

These are three drawings I created while writing my dissertation in Edinburgh. They trace my journey from the anxiety of not knowing whether I could find enough participants, to the challenge of writing the dissertation well once the data were collected, and finally to the relief of completing it successfully. I think this transition can be felt through the colours and gradients.
